title: Two-stage mixture-cure stop model: ever pit again × when
summary: A mixture-cure formulation separates failure occurrence from failure timing.
rationale: A mixture-cure formulation separates failure occurrence from failure timing. In F1 terms, many rows are effectively in a 'no more stops this race' state, and mixing those with rows that will stop soon can blur the decision boundary. Modeling 'whether another pit happens' separately from 'when it happens' is a cleaner inductive bias than a single binary next-lap model.
implementation_hint: Stage 1: classify `EverPitAgainInRace` using grouped CV and fold-safe historical features. Stage 2: on rows with future pits, fit either discrete hazard or AFT remaining-lap model. Final score is `P(EverPitAgain) * P(NextLap | EverPitAgain)`. Use current binary ensemble as either stage 1 or as a third blended expert.
expected_effect: Usually sharpens late-race and low-remaining-laps behavior, where many false positives come from cars that are on old tyres but are simply not stopping again.
risk: If `EverPitAgain` is too easy under local CV, it may look better than it generalizes; the split must be checked under race-held-out folds and preferably year-race chronological folds.
